1: Bildad's second speech opens with the standard formula. His speech is more compact than his first (ch8) and focuses entirely on the fate of the wicked, implicitly applying the portrait to Job.
2: ʿad-ʾānāh tāśîmûn qinṣê lĕmillîn: 'How long will you set snares/ends to words?' — qēṣ (end, snare) of words: either 'put an end to words' or 'set traps with words.' Tābîn wĕʾaḥar nĕdabbēr: 'get insight, and then we will speak' — Bildad demands that Job achieve understanding before speaking further.
3: Maddûaʿ neḥšabnû kabbĕhēmāh: 'Why do you count us as animals?' — bĕhēmāh (beast, animal) — the friends feel demoted by Job's critique of their wisdom. Niṭmînu bĕʿênêkem: 'we are stupid in your eyes' — ṭāmāʾ in Niphal (to be made unclean, or here, worthless) — an unusual use suggesting their perceived contemptible status.
4: Ṭōrēp napšô bĕʾappô: 'you who tear yourself apart in your anger' — the self-destructive character of Job's rage in Bildad's view. Hătaʿăzab-lĕmaʿankā ʾāreṣ: 'will the earth be forsaken for your sake?' — a cosmological challenge: does Job think his individual case will cause the moral order to collapse?
5: Gam-ʾôr rĕšāʿîm yiḏʿāk: 'the light of the wicked is extinguished' — the lamp-metaphor for life. Rĕšāʿîm (wicked ones): Bildad's term for the condemned; he is building a portrait that fits Job's situation. Šabebet ʾiššô: 'the flame of his fire' — the doubled light-image emphasizes the totality of extinction.
6: Nēr ʿālāyw yiḏʿāk: 'the lamp over him is extinguished' — nēr (lamp) as the symbol of life and divine blessing (cf. Prov 20:27: nēr YHWH nišmat ʾāḏām). When the lamp goes dark, the divine connection is severed. Dāk (extinguished) completes the double extinction begun in v5.
7: Yēṣrû ṣaʿădê ʾônô: 'his powerful strides are shortened' — ʾôn (strength, vigor) — the once-powerful man is diminished. Taššīlĕḵēhû ʿăṣātô: 'his own schemes throw him down' — self-brought ruin: the wicked man is not merely punished externally but destroyed by the internal logic of his own plans.
8: Bĕšālah bĕreget ʾĕlāyw: 'he is thrown into a net by his own feet' — rešet (net), śĕbākāh (lattice/mesh): the hunting-trap imagery. The wicked man walks into his own trap. The passive (yušlaḵ) suggests the inevitability of the outcome.
9: Yōʾḥez bĕʿāqēb pāḥ: 'a trap catches him by the heel' — pāḥ (bird-snare, trap) and ṣammîm (snare, noose) continue the hunting imagery. The heel-trap recalls Jacob/Esau's heel-motif (ʿāqab), but here there is no escape from the snare.
10: Ṭāmûn bāʾāreṣ ḥeblô: 'a rope is buried in the ground for him' — the hidden snare, pre-placed. Mallĕkōdtô ʿălê nātîb: 'his trap waits on his path' — the path the wicked man takes is itself the trap. The series of trap-images (vv8-10) accumulates to produce inevitability of capture.
11: Ballāhôt yĕbaʿătuhû sābîb: 'terrors frighten him on every side' — ballāhāh (sudden terror, dismay): the psychological dimension of the wicked man's fate. Fear surrounds completely. Hēpîṣuhû lĕraglāyw: 'they push him forward at every step' — terror drives him as a herd animal is driven.
12: Yĕhî rāʿēb ʾônô: 'his strength is starved' — an unusual idiom: rāʿēb (hungry, famished) modifying ʾôn (strength) — the vigor itself is starving, depleted. ʾĔd nākôn lĕṣalʿô: 'disaster stands ready at his side' — ʾēd (calamity, disaster) as a waiting companion.
13: Yōʾkal baḏdê ʿôrô: 'it devours the limbs of his skin' — baḏ (parts, limbs): eaten piece by piece. Bĕkôr māwet: 'firstborn of death' — a unique personification: death has a firstborn, a powerful first agent. The bĕkôr (firstborn) holds the place of greatest power and authority. Death's most powerful instrument is the disease that consumes the wicked man from within.
14: Yinnātaq mibĕṭaḥô: 'his confidence is ripped away from his home' — bāṭāḥ (confidence, security) torn loose. Melek ballāhôt: 'king of terrors' — ballāhāh (terror, horror) personified as royalty. Death enthroned as king, to whom the wicked man is brought as a prisoner. The title melek (king) applied to death anticipates NT language about the last enemy's kingship (1 Cor 15:26; Rev 20:14).
15: Tiškôn bĕʾohŏlô mibbelî lô: 'something alien inhabits his tent' — mibbelî lô (not his own): a stranger or alien force takes over the home. Gāprît tizzāreh ʿal-nāwēhû: 'brimstone is scattered over his home' — the Sodom-judgment vocabulary (Gen 19:24), the smell of divine wrath.
16: Miṭṭāḥat šārāšāyw yiḇāšû: 'his roots dry up below' — the tree metaphor for the righteous/wicked person (cf. Ps 1:3; Jer 17:8) inverted: here the wicked man's roots and branches both die. Ûmimmaʿal yimmāl qĕṣîrô: 'his branches wither above' — total death from roots to crown.
17: Ziḵrô ʾāḇad min-ʾāreṣ: 'his memory perishes from the land' — zēḵer (memory, name) as the posthumous existence: to be remembered is to continue existing in the community. The wicked man loses both. Wĕlōʾ-šēm lô ʿal-pĕnê-ḥûṣ: 'no name in the open places' — public space, where names are proclaimed (gates, marketplaces), holds no record of him.
18: Yihdĕpūhû mēʾôr el-ḥōšeḵ: 'he is driven from light into darkness' — the active expulsion: no passive fading but violent thrust from the realm of the living into the realm of the dead. Ûmitēbēl yĕniddĕhû: 'he is banished from the world' — tēbēl (the inhabited world, the civilized order) expels him. Social death precedes physical death.
19: Lōʾ nîn lô wĕlōʾ-neḵed: 'no offspring and no descendant' — nîn (offspring, son) and neḵeḏ (posterity): the total extinction of the family line. The double negative drives home the complete annihilation of legacy. No survivor (śārîd) anywhere.
20: ʿal-yômô nāšammû ʾaḥărōnîm: 'those who come after are horrified' — the wicked man becomes a horror for all future generations. Qadmōnîm ʾāḥăzû śāʿar: 'those who went before are seized with dread' — the temporal frame extends in both directions: both predecessors and successors are appalled. The portrait is universally witnessed.
21: ʾAk-ʾēlleh miškĕnôt ʿawwāl: 'surely these are the dwellings of the wicked' — the summary application. Wĕzeh mĕqôm lōʾ-yādaʿ ʾēl: 'and this is the place of the one who does not know God' — the definitional formula: the wicked man is lōʾ-yādaʿ ʾēl, one who does not know God. Yāḏaʿ (to know) in the covenantal sense: not mere intellectual awareness but covenantal relationship. The speech ends by locating the problem not in behavior but in relational status before God.
